summary: McKenzie recounts how he originally considered naming his software 'Teacher's Pet' before settling on 'Bingo Card Creator' after finding better domain names taken. He discovers that teachers-pet.org has linked to his site, driving steady referral traffic that converts at 14%, nearly matching his organic search conversion rate. The post reflects on the value of unsolicited inbound links from relevant communities.
key_points: McKenzie's naming journey went from Teacher's Pet to Bingo Card Maker to Bingo Card Creator, largely driven by domain availability
An unsolicited backlink from a small but engaged community site drove traffic comparable to his paid AdWords campaign
The referral traffic converted at 14%, close to organic search traffic, suggesting highly relevant audience targeting
Small niche community sites with engaged users can outperform their PageRank in actual referral value
money_quotes: I have since learned about good practices for naming B2C software and that idea strikes me as extraordinarily silly now.

Apparently despite the PR2 they've got quite a number of regular users, because they put up a link to me about a week ago and its getting fairly steady traffic at about the same level as my AdWords campaign, and converting pretty well, too.
Its always nice when folks take time out of their day to do your marketing for you.
key_insight: Unsolicited links from small but highly relevant community sites can drive traffic and conversions on par with paid advertising, making organic community presence invaluable for niche software businesses.
